hero_tagline: Systems That Play. Products That Last.
hero_desc: Game Designer & Product Owner with 8+ years shaping MMORPG systems — from core mechanics and economy design to full product ownership. Currently leading the team behind Silkroad Online Origin Mobile.
about_intro:
I've spent nearly a decade inside games — not just playing them, but dissecting them. I started in QA, watching players find every exploit I failed to predict. Moved into Operations, learning that a great design means nothing if the live environment can't sustain it. Then into Live-Ops Design, Game Design, and eventually Product Ownership.

This journey gave me something rare: the ability to design systems that don't just look good on paper — they survive real players, real economies, and real deadlines.
about_card1_desc: I design interconnected gameplay mechanics — quests, skills, economy, combat — that scale and evolve with the player base.
about_card2_desc: From QA to Analytics to Live-Ops, every design decision I make is informed by real player behavior and measurable outcomes.
about_card3_desc: I don't just design features. I own the product vision — from concept to launch to post-launch tuning.
about_think_quote: "A well-designed game isn't a collection of features — it's an ecosystem where every system reinforces the next. I design with the full picture in mind: quest systems that feed the economy, combat that shapes retention, monetization that enhances — not undermines — the core experience."
proj_intro: Here's where design meets reality. These are projects where I was responsible for core game systems, product direction, or both — each shaped by data, refined by player feedback, and measured by real-world results.
proj_sr_impact: "Recreated a beloved PC MMORPG for mobile. Won Game of the Year."
proj_sr_desc: A challenge that few would take on: faithfully recreating a beloved 2004 PC MMORPG for modern mobile audiences. As Game Design Lead and Product Owner, I reverse-engineered the original game's combat formula, rebuilt every core system from scratch, and led the design team through 21 months of development.
proj_yg_title:
Yulgang Private Server
Hiệp Khách Giang Hồ
proj_yg_impact: "Built a community of 780 concurrent players through thoughtful design."
proj_yg_desc: What started as nostalgia became a masterclass in game operations. I simultaneously held every role — player, QA, operator, community manager, and game designer — running a private server that reached 780 concurrent users and a 65% 7-day retention rate. More importantly, I successfully shifted the deeply traditional mindset of Yulgang players toward new systems and content.
proj_ds_impact: "Optimized a live system that lacked structure — and left it more stable than I found it."
proj_ds_desc: Reworked outdated live events and reward structures, built a clearer design metrics framework for the Game Design team, and reverse-engineered legacy balance logic to keep new hero and content updates stable over time.
think_lead: A well-designed game isn't a collection of features — it's an ecosystem where every system reinforces the others.
think_p1: When I design a quest system, I'm thinking about how it feeds into the economy. When I balance combat, I'm considering how it affects player retention. When I plan monetization, I'm ensuring it enhances — not undermines — the core experience.
think_p2: This holistic approach comes from having operated games live. I've seen firsthand what happens when systems don't talk to each other: exploits, churn, broken economies. That's why I design with the full picture in mind.
think_quote: "The best game systems are invisible — players feel the fun without seeing the architecture."
journey_intro: A concise look at my work, growth, and achievements — one lesson at a time.
j1_title: The Start — How Players Play the Game
j1_desc: Monitored player activity logs to detect abnormal accounts and provided customer support. Beyond the org's framework, independently researched player behavior — understanding how players exploit loopholes and the in-game legal system.
j1_insight: Understanding player behavior at the ground level became the foundation of every design decision I'd make later.
j2_title: The Build-Up — How a Product Runs
j2_desc: Led product planning and managed all in-game & out-of-game operations. Designed and balanced systems, content, and features as a one-person team. Developed new features later adopted by other private servers.
j3_title: The Foundation — How to Work as an Organization
j3_desc: Oversaw live operations, proposed data-driven product improvements, conducted market & community research. Transformed intuition into a data-driven mindset.
j3_photo_caption: Outstanding Employee & Project Team Award — SohaGame 2020
j4_desc: Product ops evolving into Live Ops Designer at a professional studio. Discovered a long-standing balance bug through deep gacha data analysis — a finding that had been missed for years. First exposure to structured game design processes at scale.
j5_title: The Reward — Silkroad Online Origin Mobile
j5_desc: Joined GihOt Studio in 2022 as Game Design Lead. Planned and owned the full product vision for "Recreating the Silk Road World." Designed all core systems: world, characters, stats, skills, equipment. Led design team through 3 years of development, rebalanced the full product for mobile, and fully reverse-engineered the PC version. The only individual to reconstruct the original Silkroad combat formula. Culminated in a triple win at Vietnam Game Awards 2025.
j5_photo_caption: 🏆 Vietnam Game Awards 2025 Triple Win — Game of the Year, Best Gameplay, Best Graphic Design
skills_intro: My toolkit spans the full spectrum of game production — from creative design to analytical operations.
collab_intro: I'm open to full-time roles, freelance projects, and creative collaborations. Whether you need a Game Designer, a Product Owner, or a strategic consultant — here's how we can work together.
collab_c1_desc: End-to-end system design — combat, progression, quests, economy, world-building — for new or existing titles.
collab_c2_desc: Audit and rebalance live game economies, monetization flows, and reward structures to improve retention and revenue.
collab_c3_desc: Translate beloved PC experiences to mobile — not just porting, but understanding and reimagining for the platform.
collab_c4_desc: Product vision, roadmap planning, backlog management, and stakeholder alignment for game products at any stage.
contact_sub: I'm always up for conversations about game design, product strategy, and the weird joys of balancing an in-game economy at 2 AM.
footer_tagline: Game Designer · Product Owner · Ho Chi Minh City, Vietnam
sr_ctx_p1: Silkroad Online was released in 2004. It didn't just capture a playerbase — it became a cultural reference point for an entire generation of Southeast Asian gamers who grew up grinding its routes and trading its economy. It is still remembered 20 years later.
sr_ctx_p2: The task was to rebuild it for mobile. But the conditions made it far from a standard port.
sr_ctx_l1: No design document. No formula. No blueprint left behind by the original creator.
sr_ctx_l2: Built on an internal proprietary engine — no off-the-shelf tooling or precedent to reference.
sr_ctx_l3: Game Design team: never more than 3 people at any point across 21 months.
sr_ctx_l4: 20 years of private server communities — thousands of hours of collective reverse-engineering — and still, no one had cracked the original combat formula completely.
sr_ch_title_2: But "why did they build it that way?"
sr_ch_surface_h: Rebuild a beloved game for mobile.
sr_ch_surface_p: This is the version everyone sees on the brief. Port the systems, adapt the UX for touch, preserve what made the original work. Straightforward enough — if you have the original designer's reasoning.
sr_ch_actual_h: Decode intent from 20-year-old decisions, with no author to ask.
sr_ch_actual_p: The original creator left no explanation. Only the result. Understanding why those systems were built the way they were — and whether that reasoning still held after two decades — was the real design challenge.
sr_ch_t1: 20 years of private server communities collectively tried to reverse-engineer the original combat formula. None succeeded completely. The gaps were always there. This was the baseline I inherited.
sr_ch_t2: After 20 years, was the original design still correct? Context changes. Player expectations change. What made sense for a 2004 PC playerbase may not apply to a 2022 mobile market in Southeast Asia.
sr_ch_t3: The hardest judgment call: where to follow the original creator's logic faithfully, and where to apply independent judgment where the original may have been wrong — or where time had made it wrong.
sr_ch_meta_p: Working with ambiguous direction and finding the real objective underneath the surface ask. When a boss gives vague instruction, when a document is missing, when the original decision-maker is unavailable — the ability to reconstruct intent from outcomes is the skill. This is what I was practicing for 21 months.
sr_re_title_2: Converging on what no one had solved.
sr_re_p: Community formulas existed. Players had spent years trying to crack the combat math. All of them were partially correct — accurate within certain stat ranges, wrong beyond them. No single formula held across the full progression curve.
sr_re_s1_p: Aggregate every formula variant from forums, private server wikis, and player spreadsheets spanning 20 years. Map where each was accurate and where it broke — the failure points are the data.
sr_re_s2_p: Controlled testing: ATK, DEF, every relevant stat variable across multiple character builds. Record actual outcomes. Build the raw empirical dataset that no community attempt had done systematically.
sr_re_s3_p: Treat the data as a convergence problem. Iterate: propose formula → test against recorded outcomes → identify deviation → refine → repeat until the formula holds across the full stat range.
sr_re_s4_h: Honest disclosure to team: ~90% accuracy at launch
sr_re_s4_p: Higher level = higher stat values = larger absolute deviation. The formula held cleanly at low and mid ranges, but high-level content introduced larger margins. This was disclosed to the team before launch — not buried.
sr_re_s4_callout: Why the 90% confession is a strength: Shipping with known, documented uncertainty is how good engineers work on live products. Pretending 100% confidence and then patching quietly is how trust collapses. Iterative methodology, not perfection, was the standard.
sr_re_acc_launch_sub:
Disclosed honestly to the team
before release
sr_re_acc_live_sub:
Real player data refined
the formula to full precision
sr_ec_title_1: Drop rate is not a number.
sr_ec_p1: In a free-trade MMORPG, the economy isn't a feature — it is the core loop. Every item drop, every rarity, every trading decision feeds back into why players return. The central design question had one sentence: "What do I get for 1 hour of grinding?"
sr_ec_p2: The answer had to change depending on where the player was in their journey.
sr_ec_early_p: High reward density → fast progression feel. New players need to feel momentum immediately. Frequent drops, visible level gains, early equipment upgrades — the goal is to establish the grinding loop as satisfying before the player has any investment in the world.
sr_ec_mid_p: Rewards become meaningful → longer session investment. The player now has context. An item matters because they understand what it enables. Drop density decreases, but perceived value increases. Sessions lengthen naturally because goals feel achievable but require real effort.
sr_ec_late_p: Rare items as long-term goals → trading activity → living ecosystem. Late game players aren't just grinding for themselves — they're participating in an economy. Item scarcity drives pricing, pricing drives trade, trade drives social interaction. The drop rate here isn't just a reward design decision; it's a market supply decision.
sr_ec_result: The result: grinding felt purposeful across all phases. Players stayed — and the retention numbers reflect it.
sr_fail_p: A live MMORPG with a free trade economy and an undocumented baseline is not a controlled experiment. Things broke. Here's what happened and how it was addressed — not the sanitized version.
sr_f1_p: Rare statistical combinations — high DEF builds paired with certain elemental resistance stacking — produced damage outcomes that fell outside the formula's predicted range post-launch. The formula was correct for expected play patterns; it hadn't been stress-tested against every theoretical edge case at extreme stat values.
sr_f1_fix: Addressed per edge case rather than rebuilding the formula. A formula rebuild on a live game creates a cascade of secondary balance issues. Surgical fixes, properly documented, carried lower overall risk.
sr_f2_p1: An investment package — pay upfront, receive currency returns per player level-up — was designed around expected organic leveling speed. What was not anticipated: bot farms operating at scale, leveling at speeds far beyond human players. After 30 days, bot accounts had recovered 180% of invested currency versus the actual revenue those packages should have generated. The market destabilized.
sr_f2_p2: Leveling speed and leveling difficulty had both been underestimated for non-human operators. The economic model assumed human behavior.
sr_f2_fix: Rebalanced return ratios to reflect realistic operator scaling. Split the hard currency into a separate special currency for discount investment packages — removing cross-economy trading and isolating the exploit vector from the broader market.
sr_f3_p: In a free-trade game, rare items function as publisher currency. Their scarcity is the mechanism that gives them value — and that value supports the entire in-game economy. But natural drop rates, even when deliberately low, cannot account for uncontrollable bot-volume multiplication. When bot farms run 24/7, even a 0.01% drop rate becomes a supply problem at scale.
sr_f3_fix: Redesigned the economic loop to ensure long-term market sustainability — introducing structural supply controls that accounted for non-human volume rather than assuming purely organic player activity.
sr_ls_q1: "Rebuilding a 20-year-old game isn't about copying its features. It's about understanding why those features made people choose it over everything else — and whether that reason still holds today."
sr_ls_q2: "The hardest design question isn't 'what should I build?' It's 'what was the original designer actually trying to solve — and was he right?'"
sr_ls_meta_h: The Real Skill — And Why It Transfers
sr_ls_meta_p: Most design work comes with ambiguous direction. Briefs are incomplete. Managers give you context but not intent. The original author is unavailable. This project taught me to work backward from outcomes — to reconstruct the logic behind decisions that were never documented, and to decide when that logic still applied and when it didn't. That's not a game design skill. That's a career skill.
ds_support_badge: Supporting Case Study · Live System Optimization
ds_eyebrow: Mobile RPG · Live Ops Design · GihOt Studio · 2022–2023
ds_subtitle: Optimize the live system. Build the structure that was missing. Keep it stable long-term.
ds_ctx_title_1: I joined a game already running.
ds_ctx_p1: Dark Summoner was a mobile RPG already in live operation when I joined GihOt Studio. Not a project I built from scratch — this was a running product, with real players, real content cycles, and real problems quietly building up underneath.
ds_ctx_p2: My role was Live Ops Designer — not to architect core systems, but to ensure what was running continued to run better. The work required reading the previous designer's decisions, identifying what needed fixing now, and laying groundwork so future updates wouldn't create new problems.
ds_prob1_p: Live events had been running long past their design lifespan without mechanical updates. Players knew the outcome before they started — zero engagement.
ds_prob2_p: Reward ladders no longer reflected player time-effort investment. Some modes over-rewarded, others under-rewarded — no internal system maintained the balance.
ds_prob3_p: The Game Design team had no shared metric set to evaluate designs against. Every decision was gut-feel — no objective reference point existed.
ds_prob4_p: New heroes and content were added by estimation, with no clear formula from the previous designer. Each new update introduced unpredictable balance risk.
ds_c1_p: Analyzed each live event along two axes: engagement mechanics and reward structure. For events that had lost engagement, I redesigned trigger conditions, added secondary loops with real value, and readjusted reward flow to match the current game phase.
ds_c1_callout: 💡 Principle: Events lose engagement not because rewards are too low — but because the mechanics have become too familiar. Change the loop, not just the number.
ds_c2_p: Audited the interface of key systems to identify friction points: hidden reward information, unclear tier thresholds, inconsistent feedback. Restructured reward ladders in select systems to create cleaner progression perception — so players knew exactly what they were earning and what came next.
ds_c3_p: Created an internal metric set so the Game Design team had a shared foundation when evaluating new designs. The framework covered: engagement benchmarks by system type, reward density standards by game phase, and a pre-launch event review checklist.
ds_c3_callout: 📊 Why this mattered: Without a framework, each designer held a different standard. The result was inconsistent designs and prolonged reviews. A framework creates a shared language.
ds_c3_badge: 📋 Framework became onboarding material for new designers
ds_c4_p1: This was the hardest part. The previous designer left no documentation on how they calculated hero stats and power scaling. I analyzed each existing hero — stats, skills, cost — and gradually reconstructed the original formula from patterns in the data.
ds_c4_p2: The output was a reusable spreadsheet for designing new heroes within relative power budget — maintaining consistency even though I hadn't created the original system.
ds_impact_title_2: But it's what keeps a game alive.
ds_imp1_p: Live events could be updated and deployed faster once the team had clear structure to work from.
ds_imp2_p: The metrics framework created shared standards — faster reviews, fewer gut-feel debates.
ds_imp3_p: The balance formula meant future heroes could be designed correctly from day one, not patched post-launch.
ds_lesson_title_2: From a game I did not create.
ds_ls1: Read before writing. When inheriting a live product, the first task is understanding the previous designer's intent — even without explanation. Every system has logic, even when undocumented.
ds_ls2: Structure is a feature. The metrics framework is invisible to players. But it affects every design decision that follows. Sometimes the most important work is the work that can't be seen.
ds_ls3: Constraints clarify priorities. Not being able to redesign the whole game was a constraint. But it forced clarity: what needs fixing now, what can wait. This is a necessary skill for any live product.
yg_subtitle: Reshape the culture. Rewrite the game.
yg_ctx_p1: Yulgang is a long-standing MMORPG developed by MGame, operated in Vietnam by VTC. Yulgang Private refers to unofficial, community-run servers built on Yulgang's original source code — each with its own design philosophy and operational strategy.
yg_ctx_p2: I entered the Yulgang private scene in 2017 as a dedicated player. By 2018, I had moved through QA/QC into an official partner role — and ultimately took full ownership of game design and product decisions.
yg_ch1_p: When I arrived, every Yulgang private server followed the same formula — same systems, same events, same player loops. Innovation was absent. The result was rapid churn and a community increasingly bored of a game they still loved.
yg_ch2_p: I carried two core principles into this work: challenge outdated gaming mindsets, and treat each iteration as an A/B test. This meant doing what others didn't dare — and being willing to fail publicly, and loudly, before finding what worked.
yg_ph_intro: Each phase represented a fundamentally different design philosophy — and together, they shaped how I think about game design today.
yg_ph1_p: My early servers focused on recalibrating ratios, stats, and in-game values to create a more engaging experience than the original. Incremental. Precise. Player-tested.
yg_ph1_lesson: How to read what players need — not just what they say they want.
yg_ph2_p: Radical experimentation — modifying Yulgang's core to embed ideas from Phong Thần, Võ Lâm, Kiếm Thế, MU, and Laplace M. This phase was about creating new experiences, not improving existing ones.
yg_ph2_lesson: How to create player experiences that didn't exist yet — and get a community to follow.
yg_dd_intro: Not a feature list — a set of observations turned into design.
yg_dd1_h: Turning a Developer's Mistake Into a Core System
yg_dd1_s1: Most private servers disabled the quest system entirely. Low experience rewards made it seem worthless. Then a developer accidentally left it active during a test window.
yg_dd1_s2: While others saw a bug, I saw a structure. Not for main quests — but for cyclical, repeatable loops. The kind that create daily habits, not one-time progress.
yg_dd1_s3: I refocused the reward structure: removed experience gains, introduced power-enhancing buffs with long-term value — designed to avoid resource inflation while keeping players returning daily.
yg_dd1_callout: This is the clearest example of what I believe game design is: not inventing from scratch, but seeing what's already there — and understanding it better than anyone else in the room.
yg_dd2_s1: Players had defaulted to passive auto-botting — the path of least resistance in a resource-abundant environment. Active engagement was low. Community interaction was absent.
yg_dd2_s2: I tightened the open-world resource supply and redistributed it into dungeons — requiring active participation, time investment, and strategic decision-making. Extra dungeon entries became a monetization lever.
yg_dd2_s3: Players shifted from passive to active. Community interaction increased as dungeons became shared content. The dungeon model became the default resource strategy.
yg_dd2_callout: 📊 From 2023, an estimated 80% of Vietnamese private servers adopted dungeon-based resource distribution. This server set the pattern.
yg_dd3_h: Designing the Future — Before the Official Game Did
yg_dd3_s1: Players weren't just farming for power — they were farming for the story of discovery. The feeling of finding something rare, something others hadn't reached. Status through progression, not just stats.
yg_dd3_s2: I designed a weapon tier requiring level 160 — a hard wall that forced players to fully max their progression before unlocking special material farming. The ultimate weapon form became a community status symbol and a long-term goal.
yg_dd3_callout: 🏆 In 2021 — three years after this server — Yulgang Official introduced a similar level-160 upgrade system. The private server had designed ahead of the official game.
yg_os1_p: Expanded the original 4-slot costume system into a richer stat diversity — from basic attributes to unique character-specific abilities.
yg_os2_p: Introduced territorial upgrades, time-limited member buffs, and resource sinks — transforming guilds from labels into ecosystems with strategic depth.
yg_os3_p: First server in Vietnam to implement rebirth. Built accompanying activities to gather materials, extending endgame and creating fresh progression loops.
yg_rs_text:
Beyond the numbers, the most meaningful result was cultural: I successfully shifted the traditionally conservative Yulgang player base toward accepting new systems and a fundamentally different vision of what the game could be.

For a community known for resisting change — that was the hardest metric to move.
yg_ref1_p: The Circulatory Quest didn't come from a design sprint. It came from watching a mistake and seeing what others missed. The best insight I've had came from paying attention.
yg_ref2_h: Player behavior is the most honest spec.
yg_ref2_p: No document predicts how players will actually interact with a system. The Dungeon redesign worked because I watched auto-botting long enough to understand the root cause — not just the symptom.
yg_ref3_h: Being early is a design strategy.
yg_ref3_p: Both the Celestial-Demonic system and the Dungeon model were adopted later by the broader community. That wasn't luck — it was designing toward where the game needed to go, before the market agreed.
